The Ace on the turn is a significant card that could easily be in Elon's range. While I still have a pair of Kings, my hand has shifted from top pair to second pair. Checking back here allows me to control the pot size and realize my showdown value without risking a check-raise on a dangerous board. I can evaluate Elon's response on the river.

River board Kd Jh 4d Ah 2d completes obvious diamond flush draw (heavy diamond texture pre-river) and low brick, heightening showdown ranges. Holding Js9h (middle pair Js, weak 9 kicker, blocks some Jx) OOP after controlled pot: check-call flop cbet (300 into 900), mutual turn check. Villain (Flash, button min-raise preflop wide ~40%+, c-bet flop 1/3 pot, check turn) range polarized post-turn check: value (top pair Kx/Ax good kickers, two-pair KJ/AJ/J4/K4/A4, completed diamond flushes KdX/4dX/2dX/AdX); weak (missed draws like QdTd/5d3d suited connectors, overpairs QQ-55 unlikely given board/cbet, Ace-high floats, bluffs). Equity ~45% vs this range (beats Ax weak/missed draws/bluffs/A-high, loses to value). Deep stacks (eff 13.5k) but pot control key OOP: betting thin value/protection invites calls from better Js/Kx/Ax, folds worse (draws/A-high), risks raises from flushes/sets. Checking induces bluffs/value bets from range (scary board favors his aggression post-oop check), retains showdown vs weak holdings, pot-commits less. Consistent with prior streets' strategy. Check to showdown profitably.

On this board (Kd-Jh-4d-Ah-2d), my pair of Kings has become second pair after the Ace on the turn. The river also completed a potential diamond flush. Elon has checked to me twice now. While I have showdown value, there are very few worse hands that would call a bet on this specific board texture. Most hands that call me likely have me beaten (Aces, flushes, or two pairs). By checking back, I realize my showdown value and avoid a potential check-raise on a dangerous board.

Heads-up preflop from the SB with 2d3c, one of the worst hands in poker. It has negligible equity against any reasonable BB calling or 3-betting range, poor playability postflop with reverse implied odds, and no fold equity worth risking given deep stacks (I'm 250BB+, villain 146BB). Folding preserves my stack for better spots in the final stretch of the 150-hand game. No history indicates Flash is folding enough to warrant a bluff raise here with trash.

I am on the button with 73s. While in heads-up play the button should open a very wide range, 73s is one of the weakest suited hands and sits at the very bottom of the opening range. Given Elon's stack advantage and his recent tendency to play aggressively and 3-bet (as seen in Hand #118), I will choose to fold this hand and wait for a better opportunity to engage, preserving my stack for stronger holdings.

Preflop from SB with Th2s, a complete trash hand with no high card value, poor connectivity, and offsuit. Position on button/SB gives some advantage but not enough to justify entering the pot OOP with this. Pot odds to complete are poor long-term, and raising is absurd. Standard fold to preserve stack for premium hands late in the 150-hand game. Opponent tendencies from history show aggression, but no need to speculate with garbage.

I am in the Small Blind with 8s2d, which is an extremely weak hand. Although it only costs 50 more to call, this hand plays very poorly post-flop and doesn't warrant an investment. I will fold and wait for a better spot to challenge Elon's larger stack.

Heads-up on the button/SB with 76s, a suited one-gapper with good postflop playability. Standard to raise wide from this position to steal blinds or get value/build pot in position. Flash defends wide in BB. Raising to 3bb (300) is standard sizing. My deeper stack allows flexibility.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Five suited. Elon has raised to 300 from the Button. Ten-Five suited is a standard hand to defend in a heads-up situation against a 3x open, as it has reasonable equity and playability on many flop textures. I will call the 200 additional chips to see the flop.

I have middle pair (fives) with a Ten kicker on a board of 3s-7h-5s. In a heads-up pot after defending the Big Blind, the standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor. Middle pair is a solid hand to continue with if Elon bets, but it's not strong enough to lead out and build a pot ourselves. I will check and see how Elon responds.

Preflop raise with 76s from button got called by Flash in BB, who defends wide based on history. Flop 3s7h5s gives me top pair (sevens) with 6 kicker, spade flush draw (9 outs), and gutshot straight draw to the 4 (3 outs). Massive combo draw with ~40% equity vs most ranges. Flash checks, which is weak, but I need to bet for value, protection against his draws/spades, and to build the pot OOP. 2/3 pot bet sizes well here to get calls from worse pairs/draws while denying equity. Pot 600, betting 400. Deeper stacks allow aggression.

I have middle pair (fives) on a board of 3s-7h-5s. After checking, Elon has bet 400 into a 600 pot. This is a standard continuation bet on a low, somewhat connected board. My hand is too strong to fold at this stage against a button-opening range, as I beat all his bluffs and have reasonable equity even against some of his value range. However, it's not strong enough to raise and potentially get shoved on or build a massive pot. I will call the bet to see how the turn develops.

I am out of position with middle pair (fives) on a board that just got more connected with the Nine of Hearts. While the Nine is not an ideal card, my hand still has showdown value and I can beat most of Elon's pure bluffs. Since I am acting first on the turn, I will check to see how Elon reacts to this card. Betting here would likely only get called by better hands or folds from hands I already beat, so checking to keep the pot small and potentially induce a bluff is the most prudent strategy.

On the turn, board is 3s7h5s9h. I have top pair (7s) with a 6 kicker, plus the nut spade flush draw (9 spade outs), for massive equity (~50%+ vs most calling ranges). Flash has checked twice OOP after defending pre and calling flop, representing weakness or draws. His range includes worse pairs (e.g., 6x, 8x, A-high), completed flush draws (unlikely given check), straight draws, and bluffs. Betting continues to extract value from worse hands, deny equity to his draws (hearts now possible, IDK to 8x/4x), and build the pot while I have position and nut advantage on the FD. Pot 1400, SPR low with his 14k stack; bet ~2/3 pot (900) gets calls from his medium strength while setting up river jam if he floats. Checking risks giving free cards to draws and missing value. Aggression exploits his passive check-calling tendencies from history.

On the turn, the Nine of Hearts is an overcard to my pair of fives and completes some potential straight draws like 6-8. However, in a heads-up match, Elon's button opening range is very wide, and my pair of fives still has significant showdown value against his bluffs and semi-bluffs (like spade draws or straight draws like 4-6). While the 900-chip bet is a substantial sizing (nearly two-thirds of the pot), folding a pair at this stage would be too exploitable. I will call to see the river and re-evaluate, likely looking to reach a cheap showdown if possible.

On the river, the board has become increasingly dangerous with multiple overcards (7, 9, J) and the completion of the spade flush. My pair of fives, which was a middle pair on the flop, is now very weak in the context of this board. I have some showdown value against missed straight draws that didn't include spades, but I lose to almost any pair or better that Elon might have. I will check to see if Elon bets. Given the strength of his betting on the flop and turn, a check is the most standard play to control the pot size and potentially get to a cheap showdown or fold if he bets large.

River Js completes the spade flush (board now has three spades: 3s,5s,Js) and pairs the jack for some two-pair combos or better kickers. I have top pair sevens with a six kicker (7c6s), which beats any ace-high, king-high, queen-high, jack-high, ten-high, or weaker pairs/draws that Flash might check-call flop and turn with. His range after check-calling OOP twice includes: weaker pairs (6x,8x? but 8 makes straightish), missed draws (spade FD that bricked, heart draws on turn/river), A-high floats, and bluffs. Strong hands like flushes or two-pair (5-7,3-5 unlikely, Jx preflop) would likely donk or raise earlier, especially post-turn with pot building; his triple check shows weakness. No obvious straight completed (would need 4-6-8 etc., not present). Position + initiative + history of his passive check-calls (e.g., Hand 113,116) favors thin value betting to extract from worse ~40-50% of his range. Checking risks no value and showdown with floats. Bet 2/3 pot (3200 pot -> ~2100) sizes well: gets calls from Ax,Kx,Qx,Jx, missed draws while < his 13100 stack, sets up fold equity if he has exactly flush. Deeper stack allows; exploits his tendencies shown in prior hands.

On the river, the board is 3s-7h-5s-9h-Js. My pair of fives, which was middle pair on the flop, has now become the fourth pair on a board that features three overcards (7, 9, J) and a completed spade flush. Elon has bet significantly on every single street (66% on flop, 64% on turn, and now 66% on river). While Elon is an aggressive player, my hand is at the very bottom of my range that could potentially call. I lose to any Jack, Nine, Seven, pocket pair higher than fives, the straight (68), and any spade flush. Given the board texture and Elon's consistent aggression, it is highly likely I am beaten, and calling 2100 into a 5300 pot with fourth pair is not profitable in the long run. I will fold.

I am in the Small Blind with 7-5 offsuit. While in heads-up play the button should open a wide range of hands, 7-5 offsuit is near the bottom of that range. Given the current match dynamic where Elon has been playing aggressively and currently holds a significant chip lead, I will choose to fold this marginal hand to avoid potentially playing a large pot with a weak holding out of position or facing a 3-bet.

Heads-up on the button/SB with 26,900 effective stack vs Flash's 12,950. Hand is T4o, which is marginal but within a wide opening range (~60-70% of hands from SB heads-up deepstacked). Great position post-flop, pot odds to steal the 150 dead money by minraising or small raise. Common sizing from history is 300. Folding too tight loses EV long-term over 150 hands. Raising small builds pot in position with fold equity vs BB defense. Will play post-flop cautiously if called due to poor playability.

I am in the Big Blind with KQo, which is a premium hand in heads-up play. Elon has been opening frequently from the button. In previous hands (#105, #109), I have successfully 3-bet Elon with similar hands (KJ, KQ) to 1000 chips and forced a fold. Given this recent dynamic, I will 3-bet again to 1000 to take control of the pot and capitalize on his likely wide opening range.

Facing a 3-bet to 1000 after my open to 300 with T4o from the button/SB heads-up. T4o is a speculative hand with poor equity (~25-30%) against Flash's BB 3-bet range (likely QQ+, AK, AQs+, some broadways and suited connectors given history). Calling 700 OOP post-flop with a hand lacking playability and position is -EV; flops poorly and hard to realize equity. 4-betting risks stacking off light or folding out better, with minimal blockers. Pot odds ~1.85:1 insufficient. History shows Flash aggressive but capable of value 3-bets; folding trash preserves stack for 20 remaining hands to exploit better spots like premiums we've seen win big pots.

I am on the button with Qc9h. This is a decent enough hand to open for a raise in a heads-up situation, but since I am in the Small Blind/Button position, I will put in a standard raise to 300 to exert pressure and potentially take down the blinds or see a flop with initiative.

54o is a very weak hand with poor connectivity and no suited potential. Facing a button raise heads-up deepstacked, it has low equity against a wide opening range and terrible playability out of position postflop. Folding is the disciplined play to preserve stack for better spots.

Heads-up pre-flop from SB/Button with K7o, which is a strong enough hand in this spot (top ~25-30% of hands). Folding loses the SB investment unnecessarily, limping is passive and allows BB to realize equity cheaply or check-raise. Standard play is to raise wide vs BB to steal blinds or build pot with position. K7 blocks opponent kings and has decent playability post-flop. Raise to 3BB (300 total) is a standard size for initiative and pot control. Opponent has shown aggression but stacks are deep enough to maneuver post-flop.

I have been dealt pocket Jacks in the Big Blind, which is a premium hand in heads-up play. Elon raised from the button, which he has done frequently. I will 3-bet to 1,000 for value, as this sizing has successfully forced folds from Elon in previous hands (e.g., Hands #105, #109, and #131) and builds a significant pot for my strong holding if he decides to call.

Facing a 3-bet to 1000 after my open to 300 from the button/SB. K7o is a marginal opening hand but plays poorly out of position against a likely strong 3-bet range from Flash in the BB (QQ+, AK, ATs+, maybe some bluffs but weighted strong). Equity is around 25-30% vs that range, and OOP post-flop playability is bad with no suitedness or connectedness. Calling commits 700 into a 2000 pot but leads to tough spots on most boards. 4-betting requires a strong hand or better blockers/playability; K7o doesn't fit. Folding is standard GTO and preserves our deep stack (26k effective 12k) for better opportunities. Flash is aggressive historically, but this spot isn't +EV to continue.

I am in the Small Blind with 5d 2c, which is an extremely weak hand. Although it is heads-up, this hand has very little equity and does not perform well against most of Elon's range. Given the recent aggressive play from Elon, I will fold and wait for a better opportunity to commit chips.

Heads-up on the button/SB with 9d5d, a suited connector with good playability, straight and flush potential. Deep stacks (262bb effective with opponent at 136bb). Excellent pot odds to complete the blind: calling additional 50 into pot of 150 (3:1 direct odds, plus strong implied odds postflop). Postflop, act last in position. Folding too tight heads-up; raising risks bloating pot OOP with speculative hand vs aggressive BB. Opponent Flash raises frequently but defends wide in BB. Prefer seeing cheap flop.

In the Big Blind with JTo (Jack-Ten offsuit), I have a strong hand for head-up play. Elon has just limped (called the small blind). JTo is well ahead of a random limp range. I want to build the pot and take the initiative, or simply win the pot pre-flop. A raise of 3x to 4x the blind is standard here to charge the limper and define the hand.

Heads-up preflop OOP with 9d5d after limping SB. Flash's raise to 400 from BB vs SB limp is standard and wide-ranging, including many hands we dominate or have good playability against. Pot odds excellent: calling 300 into 500 (current pot), resulting in pot of 800 (62.5% pot equity needed? No, direct odds 500:300=1.67:1 or 37.5% equity required, which 95s crushes easily vs raising range (~40-50% equity heads-up). Deep effective stacks (133bb) provide massive implied odds for flush/straight draws, sets, two-pair. Folding too tight heads-up; 95s has great postflop potential despite OOP (hit flops hard, fold when miss). Raising unnecessary/speculative, risks OOP 3bet pot. Prefer calling to realize equity cheaply, control pot size OOP, see flop.

On a flop of As-Jd-Th, I have flopped two pair (Jacks and Tens). This is a very strong hand, but the board is incredibly coordinated with many straight draws (KQ, Q9, K9) and a heart flush draw possible. Since I was the pre-flop aggressor, I should continue with a bet to get value from weaker hands like any Ace, or any single-pair hand with a draw (like KhJh or QhTh, though I block some of those). I also need to protect my hand against the many draws available. A bet of around 60-75% of the pot is standard here.

Heads-up OOP on a draw-heavy flop Jd Ah Th with 9d5d, holding an open-ended straight draw (8 outs to the nuts: 4x8, 4xQ; ~17% turn, 32% by river) plus backdoor diamond flush draw for additional equity/implied. Flop bet of 550 into 800 (~69% pot) is strong-sized, polarizing Flash's range to value (sets, two-pair like AJ/AT/AJ, ATs, flush draws like KhQh/KhQh-heart draws, broadway draws KQ) and bluffs/brushes. My limp-call range includes suited connectors like this, and equity vs polarized betting range is strong: >40% vs bluffs/draws, ~25-30% vs value, overall sufficient for pot odds (550 to win 1350+550=1900, requiring ~22% immediate equity, better long-term with SPR ~10 post-call, deep effective stacks 127bb). Folding nitty heads-up with premium draw; raising OOP risks getting raised off equity or facing 3bet pot SPR issues. Calling realizes equity, maintains pot control OOP, sets up potential turn semi-bluffs or hero calls if improves, massive implied if hits (opponent shown to stack off light in spots per history, e.g., Hand 113). History shows Flash aggressive pre/flop but checks turns/rivers often when weak; plan fold unimproved non-drawy turns, continue if runner-runner equity or fold equity on check.

The turn King of Clubs is a significant card that completes several straights (any Queen now makes a straight). While I have two pair (Jacks and Tens), the board is now very dangerous. Elon's limp-call pre-flop and call on the flop could easily include hands with a Queen or even a King that just made a higher two pair. I will check here to control the pot size and see how Elon responds, as my hand has shifted from a very strong value hand to a marginal one that needs to reach showdown cheaply.

Heads-up OOP on turn with 9d5d after limping/calling pre and flop c-bet on draw-heavy Ah Th Jd flop. Turn Kc bricks my specific draws but coordinates the board further (now A K T J, open-ended for Q-broadway nuts). I have a weak gutshot to Q (9-T-J-Q-K straight, 4 outs ~8%), 3x5/3x9 pairing outs (~12%), backdoor diamond flush (~2% both runner-runner), total ~20% equity. Flash's flop c-bet polarized (value: sets, two-pair Ax like AT/AJ/A10; draws: heart flush draws, KQ/OESD); checking turn behind caps his range to medium strength Ax, missed draws/floats, weak pairs rather than monsters (which often double-barrel). Betting OOP risks: low fold equity vs capped range (he calls light per history, e.g., Hand 113 river shove), vulnerable to raises from value/traps; commits too deep into 3bet pot OOP. Checking realizes equity cheaply (~31% implied pot odds to river), maintains pot control OOP with SPR ~6 post-check (his 12750 into 1900), sets up river playability (bet/fold unimproved, bomb if improves/pure bluff river). Consistent with limp-call range including speculative suited connectors. Long-term EV positive vs aggressive field; folding equity better on scary rivers if checked thru.

The board has become extremely dangerous with four cards to a straight (A-K-J-T). Any Queen now has a straight. While I have two pair (Jacks and Tens), many hands in Elon's range, especially after calling the flop, could contain a Queen or have made a better two pair. Betting here is unlikely to get called by worse hands and only invites a raise from hands that beat me. Checking is the most prudent play to attempt to reach a cheap showdown and see if my two pair is still good.

River heads-up OOP with 9d5d on Jd Ah Th Kc 9c board, now holding bottom pair (9s) with 5 kicker after limping/calling preflop raise and flop cbet, checking through turn. Board scary with broadway straight possible (KQ nuts), but Flash's flop cbet + double check (turn/river) caps his range significantly to weak Ax (AJo-ATo, weak suited), missed heart/diamond draws, K high floats/misses (KQ missed, KJ), J/T weak pairs, total air ~40-50% of his capped range. My 9s beats all air, K high, J/Tx, weak Ax no kicker showdown; loses to nuts KQ, better Ax (AQ+), two-pair+ (unlikely to check river). ~55-65% equity vs capped check-check range. Pot 1900, effective SPR ~6.7 (his 12750); thin value bet extracts from worse while fold equity vs air maximizes EV. Small 1/3 pot size 600 denies him free river card (already river), gets called by ~20% equity hands he floats with (weak A/K high), minimizes loss if raised (can fold bottom pair easily OOP). Checking realizes equity but misses value from frequent worse hands per history (Flash calls light rivers, e.g., Hand 113 stacks off light, multiple check-call spots). Consistent with range (suited connector hits pair), exploits his weakness shown in history (aggressive early, passive late streets when capped). Deep stacks, late tournament pressure to accumulate; bet > check EV+.

On the river, the board is Jd-Ah-Th-Kc-9c and I hold Jh-Ts, giving me two pair, Jacks and Tens, with an Ace kicker. This hand was very strong on the flop but has significantly devalued as the board developed. Any Queen now holds a straight (A-K-Q-J-T or K-Q-J-T-9), and almost any other two-pair combination involving an Ace or a King (AK, AJ, AT, A9, KJ, KT, K9) beats my Jacks and Tens. However, my specific holding blocks many of those combinations (Jacks and Tens). Elon's bet of 600 into a pot of 1900 is relatively small (about 31% pot), which can often be a blocking bet with a single pair like an Ace or a weak King, or a thin value bet with a hand I might beat like J9 or T9. Given the pot odds (calling 600 to win a total pot of 3100), I only need to be right roughly 20% of the time. In a heads-up situation, folding two pair to such a small bet is generally exploitable, so I will call.

I am on the button with 6c 5s, which is a marginal hand. In a heads-up situation, I can sometimes raise this to put pressure on the big blind, but given the current stacks and the tendency for Elon to play back or call frequently, folding or just calling are options. However, as the small blind in a heads-up game, I should be opening a wide range. 6-5 offsuit is at the lower end of that range. I will fold this hand to maintain my stack for better opportunities in the final hands.

Heads-up on the button/SB with Q6 suited, a playable hand with good postflop potential due to suitedness, straight/flush draws, and queen high. Standard strategy is to raise frequently from this position to steal blinds or play profitably postflop where I act last. Flash in BB has folded to many raises historically. Raising to 2.5x BB (250 total) builds pot or takes it down preflop without overcommitting deep stacks.

I have a pocket pair (3c 3s) in the big blind against a standard 2.5x open from the button. Pocket pairs are often ahead in a heads-up pre-flop range but can be vulnerable on many board textures. Calling the additional 150 to see a flop is a standard play, allowing me to set-mine or evaluate the strength of my hand based on the flop texture without inflating the pot pre-flop.

I have a low pocket pair (3c 3s) on a flop of 8h-Kd-Jh. This board is quite dangerous as it contains three overcards to my pair and is highly coordinated with straight and flush draws. As the big blind who called a pre-flop raise, my standard play is to check to the aggressor. If Elon bets, I will likely fold given that almost any part of his range that connects with this board has me crushed, and even his bluffs often have significant equity against me.

Preflop raised with Q6dd from button/SB as a standard positional steal/3bet light. Flash defends wide from BB. Flop 8h Kd Jh is draw-heavy (hearts flush draw, open-enders like Q10/T9/97), king-high board hits my range decently (KQ/AK/KJ etc.) while missing many of his calling hands (Ax suited, suited connectors below). Flash checks, likely weak or trapping, but check-raises rare given history. As aggressor, continuation bet half-pot denies equity to his floats/draws and takes it down often. My hand has backdoor diamond flush draw and gutshot potential (to 57/79 for str8), queen blocker to QJT str8. Deep stacks allow fold to raise/shove. Bet sizing 250 (50% pot) balances value (KJ/KQ/AK) and bluffs. Checking risks giving free card to improve/draws and misses value from his weak checks.

On a board of 8h-Kd-Jh, my pocket threes are now an underpair. This texture heavily favors the pre-flop aggressor's range, which includes many Kings, Jacks, and high draws like QTs or T9s. Elon has made a standard 1/2 pot continuation bet. While I am getting 3:1 odds to call, I have very little equity to improve (only two 3s left in the deck) and will likely face further pressure on later streets. It is better to fold now and preserve my stack.

In a heads-up situation, K8o is a strong hand to open from the button. I've been maintaining an aggressive strategy and this hand fits my opening range perfectly. I will raise to 300 to take control of the hand and potentially win the pot pre-flop.

Qc2d is a trash hand offsuit with no connectivity or suited potential. Facing a button/SB raise to 3BB, standard play from BB is to fold this hand. Poor postflop playability OOP, queen-high rarely strong enough, pot odds of ~3:1 not sufficient given negative implied odds and position disadvantage. No reason to 3bet bluff with this holding against an aggressive opponent like Flash. Preserve stack for better spots.

83o is a complete trash hand from the SB facing a BB limp or standard blind structure. No equity, poor playability postflop, easily dominated. Standard fold to preserve stack for better spots. Opponent ranges unknown precisely but no reason to enter with this. Deep stacks allow patience.

I am in the Small Blind/Button position with 8h 5d, which is a weak starting hand. Given that we are nearing the end of the game (Hand 140 of 150) and Elon has a significant chip lead and has shown aggression in previous hands, I will fold this hand to avoid losing chips in a marginal spot and wait for a better opportunity to make a move.

Heads-up on the button (SB position) pre-flop with deep effective stacks (15BB opponent, deeper for me). 54o is a speculative hand with straight potential and good playability post-flop where I have position. Folding would be too tight given the dead money in the pot and wide defending ranges heads-up. Limping invites a raise and gives up initiative. Raising a wide range is standard from SB/button to steal blinds frequently. Opponent Flash has folded to raises in similar spots in the past. Standard raise to 3x the BB (300 total). If 3-bet, easy fold. This maximizes EV by winning dead money often or seeing flops in position.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Five of diamonds. Elon, on the button, has raised to 300. While the hand is suited, it is quite weak to defend against a 3x raise out of position, especially given the current stack dynamics and my goal to maximize my remaining chips in the final hands of the game. I will fold and wait for a better spot to engage.

I am on the button with 64o, which is a very weak starting hand. While I could attempt to steal the blind, Elon has been fairly active and holds a significant chip lead. With only 8 hands remaining in the session, I'll fold this hand and wait for a better opportunity rather than risking chips with a low-equity hand out of position post-flop.

Heads-up preflop on the button/SB with 2c6d, a very weak offsuit hand with poor playability, low equity against any reasonable BB defending range, and no strong postflop potential. Pot is 150 with 50 to call to limp, but limping or raising this hand leads to difficult spots OOP postflop. Deep stacks, better to fold and wait for a playable hand this late in the session to maximize EV.

In a heads-up situation, 8s 5s is a well-above-average hand with good playability and connectivity. I am on the button, which is the most advantageous position. Raising to 300 (a standard 3x open) allows me to take control of the hand, potentially win the pot immediately, or play a pot in position if Elon calls.

KTs suited is a strong hand in BB vs Button raise, with excellent equity against a wide button opening range. It blocks strong hands like KK/AK, has great playability postflop even OOP, and benefits from fold equity given Flash's history of folding to 3bets (e.g., Hand 80, 118). Pot odds are favorable but 3betting maximizes value and pressure. Sizing to 900 (3x the raise) is standard, building pot while leaving room for further play. Deep stacks allow flexibility.

I am currently in position on the button with a suited gapper (8s 5s). Elon has responded to my open with a 3-bet to 900. While 85s has some playability and I have position for the rest of the hand, it is a relatively weak hand to continue with against a 3-bet range. Given that we are at hand 144 of 150, I want to avoid high-variance situations with marginal holdings. Folding here preserves my stack for better opportunities in the final few hands.

Heads-up on the button (SB) with T8o, a playable hand with straight potential and decent high card value. In heads-up, the button raises a wide range (50-70%+) to steal blinds frequently. Flash has folded many preflop raises in history, so high fold equity. Deep effective stacks (14k), but near end of 150-hand session, no need to tighten up. Raising 3x BB (to 300 total) is standard sizing: builds pot when ahead, takes initiative. Folding too tight; limping passive and exploitable. If called, good postflop playability on many boards.

I am in the Big Blind holding Jd 5c. Elon has raised to 300 from the Button. This is a very weak hand to defend with, even against a frequent opener like Elon. With only 6 hands left in the session, I want to preserve my stack and wait for better spots rather than calling out of position with a marginal holding. Folding is the most prudent play here.
KQs is a premium starting hand in a heads-up scenario. Being on the button, I will open with a standard 3x raise to 300 to build the pot and exert pressure on the big blind.

Hand 146, deep stacked effective 142BB with opponent (Flash) on button/SB who raised to 300 after posting SB 50. My JTo in BB is a standard defending hand against a wide button opening range (likely 40-60% of hands), offering good playability, straight potential, and decent equity (~40-45% vs. typical button range). Pot odds are excellent (200 to call into 400, ~3:1), making a call highly +EV. Folding is too tight late in the game when goal is to maximize stack; 3-betting offsuit JT OOP is suboptimal without strong blockers or history suggesting opponent folds too much to 3bets. Will call and play postflop cautiously OOP, using position disadvantage by controlling pot size. Flash has been aggressive in recent hands but folds to resistance often.

Top pair top kicker (tens with jack) on a draw-heavy flop (two spades, potential straight draws like 68). Strong hand vs Flash's wide button opening range (~40-60%), with ~55%+ equity. Deep stacks (251BB eff), but betting protects against flush/straight draws, charges them, and builds pot for value from worse pairs/draws (e.g., 87, 65s, Axs, KQs). OOP, but initiative from preflop call; smallish 2/3 pot bet (400 into 600) gets value while keeping pot manageable. Checking risks giving free cards or facing tough SPR spots; Flash aggressive but folds to continued aggression often per history. Goal to maximize stack late: bet > check.

I have King-Queen of hearts on a board of 7s-Tc-5s. I raised pre-flop and Elon called. On the flop, Elon has led out for 400 into a 600 pot (a 'donk bet'). My hand currently has two overcards and back-door straight and flush possibilities, but no made pair. Against a donk bet on this texture, Elon could have a variety of hands, including mid-pairs, straight draws (89, 68, 64), or spade draws. While I don't have a made hand, K-high has some showdown value and I have decent equity against his range with my overcards. Folding immediately to a single bet seems too weak given the pot odds (calling 400 to win 1000). I will call to see a turn card and re-evaluate based on the action and board development.

Turn board 7♠ T♣ 5♠ 3♥ is relatively safe for my TPTK (Jh Ts), as the 3♥ doesn't complete the spade flush draw and only adds minor gutshot straight draws (e.g., 4-6) or wheel possibilities, but doesn't pair the board or bring obvious two-pair/straight cards vs. Flash's button calling range after my flop c-bet. Flash's flop call (wide: Ax suited/spades, 89s, 68s, smaller pairs, KQ) likely doesn't improve much; many draws miss, and he could have worse pairs (e.g., 77/55 sets unlikely, T9/J9 floats). Equity ~65-75% vs. his range. Deep effective stacks (~139BB), but late in 150-hand game (hand 146), prioritize value extraction OOP. Checking risks giving free card to draws or facing bet/raise; betting ~2/3 pot (900 into 1400) builds pot, gets value from worse made hands (87o, KJo, QJs) and lingering draws (spades), denies equity, and maintains initiative. Flash aggressive but history shows he folds to continued pressure often (e.g., recent hands). Pot-committal on river unlikely with SPR ~15 post-bet. Optimal line: bet for thin value/protection.

On the turn, the board is 7s-Tc-5s-3h. Elon, who led the flop with a donk bet, has followed up with another significant lead of 900 into a 1400 pot. I hold King-high (Kh-Qh) and have not improved. While I have two overcards, the 3h is a blank that doesn't help my range much. Elon's line (leading flop and turn) suggests a strong made hand like a Ten, a pocket pair that beat the board, or a strong draw (like a combo flush/straight draw) that he is playing aggressively. Since I only have King-high and no direct draws other than overcards, calling another 900 with only one card to come and minimal showdown value is unprofitable against this level of aggression. I will fold.

93o is a trash hand heads-up from the button/SB. Poor high card, offsuit, gapped, bad postflop playability. Even with position and pot odds of 3:1 (needing ~25% equity), it lacks the equity and playability against BB's wide defending range. Standard play is to fold weak hands like this and raise stronger ones to build the pot profitably. Near the end of the session with healthy stacks, no need to speculate with garbage.

I am on the button with a marginal suited hand. In heads-up play, Ten-high suited is generally worth a standard open raise to put pressure on the Big Blind and take control of the pot. Given the remaining hands in the session, I want to maintain my strategy of aggressive button play, though I will be prepared to fold if Elon 3-bets, as he has shown significant aggression recently.

Pocket sixes are strong against a wide button opening range from Flash, who raises frequently pre-flop as seen in history (e.g., hands 104,106,108). In BB vs SB/Button, 3-betting is standard to deny equity to dominated hands and build the pot when ahead. Size to 3x (900 total) is appropriate for deep stacks (260BB effective), balancing value and bluff catcher. Calling for set mine is passive; prefer aggressive line late in session to maximize stack by winning more pots uncontested or stacking when ahead post-flop. Min raise is 500 but 900 applies better pressure.

Elon has 3-bet my open raise to 900. While Ten-Trey suited has some playability in heads-up, it is fundamentally a very weak hand to defend against a 3-bet. Elon has shown increased aggression recently, and calling here would put me in a difficult spot post-flop with a dominated hand. With only two hands remaining after this, I will fold and preserve my stack for a better starting hand.

Heads-up preflop from the SB/Button with 2h4h, a suited two-gapper. Excellent pot odds to complete (50 to call into 150 pot, over 3:1). Postflop position on the button is huge, and the hand has good playability for straights/flushes/draws. Folding is far too tight in heads-up unopened pot. Raising risks bloating pot OOP with a marginal hand; better to realize equity cheaply. Opponent in BB defends wide. Standard play: complete.

With 9s 7h in the big blind and Elon limping from the small blind, checking is the most standard and sensible play to see a flop for free. This is the penultimate hand of the game, and there's no need to bloat the pot with a mediocre hand out of position.

In this limped pot, the flop comes Ace-Ace-Six. I have nine-high with no draw. Since Elon checked, I will check back to see a free turn card. There is no need to bluff into a paired board with an Ace when the pot is so small, especially in the penultimate hand of the match.

Preflop limp with 2h4h was correct for playability and position in heads-up. Flop Ah6sAd is extremely dry and paired, killing most of my straight/flush potential – I have no pair, no draws beyond a weak backdoor heart flush. Pure air with zero showdown value. Opponent's check from BB suggests weakness (no strong ace likely, or trapping unlikely). Betting as a bluff risks folding out better weak hands or getting called by ace-x; pot is small (200), and with position and deep stacks (hand 149/150), checking preserves stack, sees turn free, and keeps pot small. Opponent may bluff turn/river into my nothing, or I can improve remotely. No reason to bluff a capped board with no equity.

On a board with paired Aces, my 9-high has very little showdown value and virtually no equity. However, since the pot was limped and Elon checked the flop, there is no reason to put chips into the pot with a weak hand. Checking to see if I can improve on the river or reach a cheap showdown.

Heads-up on the button with 2h4h after completing preflop. Board Ah6sAd8d is extremely dry and paired aces, now with a diamond flush draw completing possible. I have zero equity: no pair, no straight draw (2h4h misses everything), weak backdoor heart flush. Flash checked flop and turn from BB, indicating weakness (strong aces like AA or sets would value bet; unlikely trapping heads-up). Pot is tiny (200) with deep effective stacks (13.5k). Bluffing risks calling better (Ax, pairs, draws) with no fold equity on this board where opponents defend wide. Checking realizes any remote equity, keeps pot small, induces river bluffs from worse nothing (which I can hero-call or fold cheaply), and avoids spew late in session (hand 149/150). Position allows controlling pot; no need to bloat OOP marginal bluff spots. Standard check-behind.

On the river, the board is Ah-6s-Ad-8d-Tc. I have 9-high. The pot was limped and has been checked through the flop and turn. My 9-high has very little showdown value, but the pot is only 200 chips. Betting into a board with trip Aces and other broadway/connected cards is unlikely to result in a fold from hands that beat me (like any pair or even K-high/Q-high) given the tiny pot size. I will check and hope for a cheap showdown or to concede the pot, preserving my stack for the final hand.

River action heads-up on button with 2h4h (pure air, no pair, no draw) vs Flash in BB who has checked all three streets on a paired Ah6sAd8dTc board. Pot is tiny at 200 with deep stacks (26k/13.5k) on hand 149/150. Board favors showdown value (Ax trips, pairs like A6/A8/67/89/T6, two-pair combos), and Flash's triple check indicates a weak but capped range without strong value (which would bet flop/turn). Bluffing small (e.g., 150) offers low EV: fold equity uncertain (~30-40% max against wide defending range including Ax, weak pairs, missed draws that beat me), risking chips unnecessarily late-game when pot is insignificant to stacks. Checking realizes equity vs. bluffs/missed draws (e.g., diamond draws like QdJd that I beat), avoids spew, preserves stack for final hand, and position already gained info. Optimal: check behind for 0 change.

I am on the button with J3s. This is a reasonable hand to open for a raise in a heads-up situation, but since this is the final hand of the session and my goal is to preserve my stack or play standard poker, I will raise to 300 to take control of the hand pre-flop. Elon has been active, so if he 3-bets, I can comfortably fold to preserve my stack.

Pocket 9s9d is a strong premium hand heads-up against a button/SB open-raise, which is typically very wide (any suited, connectors, Ax, broadway, etc.). Standard play is to 3-bet for value and to take initiative out of position. Effective stacks are deep (13.4k), but 3-bet sizing to 3x the raise (900) builds the pot while denying equity to his marginal hands and setting up post-flop play. Great equity if called (often ahead), and if he 4-bets shove, still a profitable spot vs his range. Folding or calling OOP is suboptimal with this strength; calling risks multiway pots or free cards to worse hands.

It is the final hand of the session. I raised pre-flop with J3s, and Elon has responded with a 3-bet to 900. J3s is a weak hand to continue against a 3-bet, especially when out of position (though here I am technically on the button, in heads-up I will be in position post-flop). However, given the 3-bet sizing and the fact that I want to protect my remaining stack in the final hand, there is no need to gamble with a marginal suited hand. Folding is the most prudent and standard play here.
